message: The cancel button is only enabled when `_cts` is not `null`. Either `!` or `?` just ends up silencing the compiler.

Maybe it is worth noting that support for timeouts is half-baked. Despite having TimeoutException in case of timeout regular cancellation exception is thrown so on surface it is not possible to tell what happened. Of course you can wrap the code with try-catch check which token triggered exception and switch the exceptions but still it is pity this is not built-in logic.
My mistake, I was not aware of the existence of the "null-forgiving operator". Thanks for the quick reply.

The cleanup I was referring to in my post is the list of registrations, which is cleared both on `Dispose` and on `Cancel`.

You are correct, though, in that a `Cancel` of a linked cancellation token source does not free the registration from its "parent", while a `Dispose` does. I'll update this post to reflect that; thanks!

Do you have an example on how to cancel/timeout/kill a thread-blocking method, such as Thread.Sleep(TimeSpan.FromHours(1))? 

I've run into a situation where I need to forcibly timeout a method that is blocking the thread, but CancellationTokenSource doesn't appear to be my best option since I can't check for whether the token is cancelled. 

Is wrapping it in a Thread(), and doing a Thread.Abort() after a desired period of time my only recourse?

I do have plans to finish this series (someday!). The last entry will be "how to cancel uncancelable code". But first, try as hard as you can to avoid that situation completely: can you add any kind of periodic check for a cancellation token? I've seen code that has to do blocking waits add short timeouts (e.g., 100ms or 1s) just to check for cancellation. If possible, that's the easiest solution.

But some code is just uncancelable. The only safe solution in that case is to isolate the code into a separate process (separate threads do not provide sufficient isolation). Then you enter a whole world of kicking off the process, two-way messaging via inter-process communication, etc. It's a real pain but it's the only safe way to cancel uncancelable code.
